steps.title: Welcome to Day 25 — Myth-Busting Day
steps.body: A lot of scary headlines surround AI. Today we look at the most common fears and separate fact from fiction. By the end, you will have a clearer, calmer view of what AI actually is — and what it is not.

steps.title: You have probably heard this
steps.body: "AI will take my job." "AI is smarter than doctors." "AI knows what I'm thinking." "If AI is wrong, no one can tell." These ideas spread fast — but most of them are either exaggerated or simply untrue. Let's look at the real picture.

steps.title: Myth 1: AI will take all jobs
steps.body: History tells a different story. Every major technology — from the printing press to the computer — shifted what people do, not how many people work. AI is doing the same thing. Routine tasks get handed to AI; human judgment, creativity, and relationships become more valuable than ever.

steps.title: Jobs and AI
steps.question: What does history suggest about new technology and jobs?
options: Technology always eliminates more jobs than it creates
Technology tends to shift the type of work people do, not eliminate work entirely
Technology has no effect on employment
Technology only creates jobs for engineers
feedback: This fear is understandable, but history shows a more balanced outcome — jobs change more than they disappear.
Exactly. The nature of work evolves. New roles appear as old ones change.
Technology does affect work — it transforms what skills are needed most.
That has not been the pattern. Many industries and roles benefit from new technology.

steps.title: What AI shifts in your role
steps.body: Think about your own work. Tasks AI handles well: sorting and summarizing information, drafting first versions, answering common questions. Tasks that stay human: judging nuance, building trust, making ethical decisions, leading people through change.

steps.title: Myth 2: AI is always right
steps.body: AI can be confidently wrong. It "hallucinates" — that means it generates text that sounds authoritative but contains made-up facts, wrong dates, or invented sources. This is not a bug that will be fully fixed. It is a built-in trait of how AI works. Your job is to verify anything that matters.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: AI can produce incorrect information that sounds completely convincing.
feedback: Actually, this is true — AI can be confidently wrong. Always check important facts.
Correct. Sounding confident and being correct are two different things when it comes to AI.

steps.title: Handling AI errors
steps.question: A colleague says AI told them a surprising fact. What is the best response?
options: Trust it — AI would not make that up
Ignore it — AI is always wrong
Verify it with a reliable source before acting on it
Ask AI the same question again and accept the second answer
feedback: AI can sound very confident while being completely wrong. Trust, but verify.
AI is often very helpful — the goal is smart verification, not blanket distrust.
Yes. A quick check against a credible source is the right habit.
Asking again can give a different answer, but neither is guaranteed to be correct.

steps.title: When hallucinations happen
steps.body: A lawyer once submitted an AI-written brief that cited court cases that did not exist. The AI invented them. The lawyer did not check. This is a real story — and a real reminder. AI is a powerful first draft, not a final authority.

steps.title: Myth 3: AI is conscious
steps.body: AI does not think, feel, want, or understand. It processes patterns in text and generates statistically likely responses. When it says "I feel excited about this," it is not experiencing excitement — it is producing words that fit the pattern. There is no one home.

steps.title: True or false?
steps.question: When an AI says it is excited to help you, it is genuinely experiencing excitement.
feedback: Correct. AI produces words that fit patterns. It does not have feelings or experiences.
This is actually false. AI generates text that sounds emotional, but there is no experience behind it.

steps.title: Good use of AI?
steps.question: Ask AI to decide whether you should quit your job by explaining all your personal feelings and asking it to make the call for you.
feedback: That is the right call. AI can help you think through options and pros and cons, but the final judgment on a life decision belongs to you.
Correct. AI is a thinking partner, not a decision-maker for choices that affect your life deeply.

steps.title: Myth 4: AI replaces human expertise
steps.body: AI augments expertise — it does not replace it. A doctor who uses AI to review scan results is more effective. A lawyer who uses AI to draft briefs is faster. A teacher who uses AI to personalize lessons reaches more students. The expert stays in the loop. The expert makes the call.

steps.title: AI and expertise
steps.question: How does AI work best alongside human experts?
options: AI handles everything and the expert signs off without reviewing
The expert uses AI to work faster and smarter while keeping judgment and accountability
AI replaces the expert for all routine work
The expert does all the work and AI is only used for final formatting
feedback: Signing off without reviewing defeats the purpose. The expert's judgment is what makes AI safe to use.
Exactly. AI raises the floor of what an expert can produce, while the expert provides the judgment.
Even in routine cases, the expert's review catches AI errors that could cause real harm.
That leaves a lot of value on the table. AI can assist throughout the whole process.

steps.title: Good use of AI?
steps.question: Use AI to draft a first version of a project proposal, then review and refine it yourself before sending.
feedback: This is a great use of AI — it speeds up drafting while keeping your expertise in charge.
Yes. AI drafts, you judge. That is the winning combination.

steps.title: The four myths in one view
steps.body: Myth 1: AI takes all jobs. Truth: AI shifts roles. Myth 2: AI is always right. Truth: AI hallucinates — verify what matters. Myth 3: AI is conscious. Truth: AI processes patterns, not feelings. Myth 4: AI replaces experts. Truth: AI amplifies experts.
steps.primaryButton: I see the full picture now

questions.question: What does AI "hallucination" mean?
options: AI sees images that are not there
AI generates confident but incorrect or made-up information
AI becomes confused when given too much input
AI repeats the same answer in a loop

questions.question: Technology throughout history has generally shifted jobs rather than permanently eliminating all work.
When AI expresses feelings like excitement or curiosity, what is actually happening?
options: AI is genuinely experiencing those emotions
AI is generating words that statistically fit the conversation pattern
AI is testing whether the user will be fooled
AI is reflecting the user's emotions back at them

questions.question: AI works best by ______ human expertise, not replacing it.

steps.title: Day 25 complete — Myth Buster
steps.body: You now have a calm, clear view of AI. You know what it can and cannot do, why you should always verify important facts, and why your human judgment is more valuable than ever. Tomorrow: building the daily AI habits that stick.
